# jj Git Colocated Interoperability Checkpoints ## Background You are an OS engineer evaluating Jujutsu (`jj`) as a drop-in workflow alongside Git. A colocated repository lets you run both `jj` and `git` commands in the same directory — `jj` stores its metadata in `.jj/` while Git keeps its `.git/` directory at the root, and the two stay synchronized automatically. In this task you will take an **existing pure-Git repository**, layer `jj` on top of it using `jj git init --colocate`, build a small commit history, create a bookmark (jj's equivalent of a Git branch), verify that the bookmark is visible as a Git branch via `git`, then delete the bookmark in jj and confirm that Git also reflects the deletion. The starting environment contains: - A directory `/home/user/gitrepo` that is already a plain Git repository with two commits: `initial commit` (adding `README.md`) and `add config` (adding `config.toml`). - The Git `HEAD` points to the `main` branch. --- ## Your Tasks ### 1. Initialize jj in colocated mode Change into `/home/user/gitrepo` and initialize a jj repository in **colocated** mode so that both `jj` and `git` commands work inside the same directory. After initialization, confirm that: - A `.jj/` directory now exists inside `/home/user/gitrepo`. - `jj status` exits successfully (exit code 0). - `jj log` shows the two existing commits inherited from Git. ### 2. Configure jj user identity Set the jj user identity at the **repo level** using `jj config set`: - `user.name` → `"OS Engineer"` - `user.email` → `"os@example.com"` These must be set with the `--repo` flag so they apply only to this repository. ### 3. Create a new commit Using only jj commands: 1. Create a new file `/home/user/gitrepo/notes.txt` with the single line `interop test`. 2. Describe (commit) the current working-copy change with the message `"add notes"`. Verify that `jj log` now shows a third commit with description `add notes`. ### 4. Create a jj bookmark and verify it appears as a Git branch 1. Create a bookmark named `feature/interop` pointing to the current working-copy commit (`@`) using `jj bookmark create`. 2. Run `jj git export` to push jj's state into the underlying Git repository. 3. Verify the bookmark appears as a Git branch by running: ``` git -C /home/user/gitrepo branch ``` The branch `feature/interop` must appear in that output. ### 5. Move the bookmark forward 1. Create another new file `/home/user/gitrepo/notes2.txt` with the content `second note`. 2. Commit the working-copy change with `jj commit -m "add notes2"`. 3. Move the `feature/interop` bookmark so that it now points to the new working-copy commit (`@-` is the just-committed change; you want to point at `@-` which is the commit you just finalized with `jj commit`). - Use `jj bookmark move feature/interop --to @-`. 4. Export to Git again with `jj git export`. 5. Verify with `git -C /home/user/gitrepo log --oneline feature/interop` that the branch tip has description `add notes2`. ### 6. Delete the bookmark in jj and confirm Git reflects the deletion 1. Delete the `feature/interop` bookmark with `jj bookmark delete feature/interop`. 2. Export to Git with `jj git export`. 3. Confirm that the branch no longer appears in `git -C /home/user/gitrepo branch`. --- ## Verification conditions At the end of the task, the following must all be true: 1. `/home/user/gitrepo/.jj` directory exists (colocated repo was initialized). 2. Running `jj log -r 'description("add notes2")' --no-graph -T 'description'` inside `/home/user/gitrepo` prints `add notes2`. 3. The file `/home/user/gitrepo/notes.txt` exists and contains the line `interop test`. 4. The file `/home/user/gitrepo/notes2.txt` exists and contains the line `second note`. 5. The bookmark `feature/interop` does **not** exist in jj (deleted): `jj bookmark list` should not include `feature/interop`. 6. The Git branch `feature/interop` does **not** exist: `git branch` inside the repo should not list it. 7. The jj repo-level config has `user.name = "OS Engineer"` (verifiable via `jj config get --repo user.name`).   

Pochi

A colocated repository lets you run both `jj` and `git` commands in the same directory — `jj` stores its metadata in `.jj/` while Git keeps its `.git/` directory at the root, and the two stay synchronized automatically.\n\nIn this task you will take an **existing pure-Git repository**, layer `jj` on top of it using `jj git init --colocate`, build a small commit history, create a bookmark (jj's equivalent of a Git branch), verify that the bookmark is visible as a Git branch via `git`, then delete the bookmark in jj and confirm that Git also reflects the deletion.\n\nThe starting environment contains:\n- A directory `/home/user/gitrepo` that is already a plain Git repository with two commits: `initial commit` (adding `README.md`) and `add config` (adding `config.toml`).\n- The Git `HEAD` points to the `main` branch.\n\n---\n\n## Your Tasks\n\n### 1. Initialize jj in colocated mode\n\nChange into `/home/user/gitrepo` and initialize a jj repository in **colocated** mode so that both `jj` and `git` commands work inside the same directory.\n\nAfter initialization, confirm that:\n- A `.jj/` directory now exists inside `/home/user/gitrepo`.\n- `jj status` exits successfully (exit code 0).\n- `jj log` shows the two existing commits inherited from Git.\n\n### 2. Configure jj user identity\n\nSet the jj user identity at the **repo level** using `jj config set`:\n- `user.name` → `\"OS Engineer\"`\n- `user.email` → `\"os@example.com\"`\n\nThese must be set with the `--repo` flag so they apply only to this repository.\n\n### 3. Create a new commit\n\nUsing only jj commands:\n1. Create a new file `/home/user/gitrepo/notes.txt` with the single line `interop test`.\n2. Describe (commit) the current working-copy change with the message `\"add notes\"`.\n\nVerify that `jj log` now shows a third commit with description `add notes`.\n\n### 4. Create a jj bookmark and verify it appears as a Git branch\n\n1. Create a bookmark named `feature/interop` pointing to the current working-copy commit (`@`) using `jj bookmark create`.\n2. Run `jj git export` to push jj's state into the underlying Git repository.\n3. Verify the bookmark appears as a Git branch by running:\n   ```\n   git -C /home/user/gitrepo branch\n   ```\n   The branch `feature/interop` must appear in that output.\n\n### 5. Move the bookmark forward\n\n1. Create another new file `/home/user/gitrepo/notes2.txt` with the content `second note`.\n2. Commit the working-copy change with `jj commit -m \"add notes2\"`.\n3. Move the `feature/interop` bookmark so that it now points to the new working-copy commit (`@-` is the just-committed change; you want to point at `@-` which is the commit you just finalized with `jj commit`).\n   - Use `jj bookmark move feature/interop --to @-`.\n4. Export to Git again with `jj git export`.\n5. Verify with `git -C /home/user/gitrepo log --oneline feature/interop` that the branch tip has description `add notes2`.\n\n### 6. Delete the bookmark in jj and confirm Git reflects the deletion\n\n1. Delete the `feature/interop` bookmark with `jj bookmark delete feature/interop`.\n2. Export to Git with `jj git export`.\n3. Confirm that the branch no longer appears in `git -C /home/user/gitrepo branch`.\n\n---\n\n## Verification conditions\n\nAt the end of the task, the following must all be true:\n\n1. `/home/user/gitrepo/.jj` directory exists (colocated repo was initialized).\n2. Running `jj log -r 'description(\"add notes2\")' --no-graph -T 'description'` inside `/home/user/gitrepo` prints `add notes2`.\n3. The file `/home/user/gitrepo/notes.txt` exists and contains the line `interop test`.\n4. The file `/home/user/gitrepo/notes2.txt` exists and contains the line `second note`.\n5. The bookmark `feature/interop` does **not** exist in jj (deleted): `jj bookmark list` should not include `feature/interop`.\n6. The Git branch `feature/interop` does **not** exist: `git branch` inside the repo should not list it.\n7. The jj repo-level config has `user.name = \"OS Engineer\"` (verifiable via `jj config get --repo user.name`)."}]}
